nativeRange.habitat: The Corky Sea Finger is found on shallow tropical Caribbean reefs, back-reef rubble zones, seagrass-margin patches, and reef walls, typically at depths from 1 to 40 meters. In the wild, colonies attach to dead coral skeleton, bare rock, or rubble, where the encrusting holdfast spreads as a thin purple-grey mat from which erect, cylindrical finger lobes project upward.

decorPreferences: live rock base for anchoring the encrusting holdfast
open vertical space above the placement point to accommodate finger-lobe growth
mid-tank or lower-mid placement with unobstructed light from above
spacing buffer from neighboring corals (encrusting base can spread laterally over adjacent rock)
moderate-to-strong indirect flow across the colony to keep polyps clean

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae and generally thrives on light alone in a well-lit reef tank. Has been documented as an active polyp feeder and is commonly reported to benefit from occasional supplemental feeding of phytoplankton, rotifers, oyster eggs, or commercial reef-coral foods to support growth and polyp extension. Supplemental iodine, calcium, and trace-element dosing is commonly advised alongside regular water changes.

avoidWith: slow-growing SPS corals placed within reach of the encrusting base
expensive LPS corals adjacent to the spreading mat
shaded or low-flow placements (polyps tend to retract for prolonged periods)
any coral placed on connected rockwork without a sand or epoxy barrier (the encrusting base can colonize neighboring rock surfaces)

summary: The Corky Sea Finger (Briareum asbestinum) is a Caribbean soft coral with a distinctive growth form: a thin encrusting base sends up erect, cylindrical finger lobes covered in long, grass-like brown-green polyps. It is generally considered a good beginner gorgonian and is primarily photosynthetic.
careNotes:
Species distinction — important: The Corky Sea Finger (Briareum asbestinum) is a Caribbean soft coral with an upright, stalked growth form, and is taxonomically and visually distinct from the Indo-Pacific encrusting Green Star Polyp form (Briareum violaceum, see [green-star-polyp-coral]). Both share the Briareum genus and form a thin encrusting base, but B. asbestinum is defined by its erect, cylindrical, unbranched finger lobes that project upward from the holdfast — typically reaching around 30 cm in the wild, with exceptional specimens reportedly approaching a meter in height. Finger lobes are typically purple, grey, tan, brown, or greenish-brown, with long, grass-like brown-green polyps that create a furry, hair-like surface texture when fully extended.

Lighting: Generally considered to do well across a moderate-to-high lighting range; published guidance commonly clusters around 50–150 PAR, with reports that the species extends polyps more readily under moderate light than under very intense direct illumination. It is broadly regarded as forgiving across a range of modern reef lighting setups.

Flow: Has been reported to do best in moderate-to-strong indirect flow. Direct laminar high flow can cause persistent polyp retraction; turbulent or alternating flow patterns are generally preferred. Brief polyp closure after a water change, lighting adjustment, or placement change is normal and not a cause for concern.

Placement and growth: Best placed in the mid-to-lower zone with open vertical space above the colony to allow finger-lobe expansion. The encrusting base behaves similarly to a slow-spreading GSP-style mat — over time it can colonize rock surfaces it contacts, so containment is worth planning from the start: isolating the colony on a dedicated frag rock or rock island, or using an epoxy or glue barrier, can slow lateral spread. Spread is generally reported to be slower than B. violaceum but still substantial over months.

Water chemistry: Reef-standard parameters apply. Temperature roughly 24–27 °C, pH 8.1–8.4, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, calcium approximately 400–450 ppm, magnesium approximately 1,250–1,350 ppm, salinity around SG 1.023–1.026. Like most octocorals, regular iodine and trace-element dosing is commonly advised alongside routine water changes.

Reef safety: Generally considered reef-safe, but classified as semi-aggressive because the encrusting base can overgrow adjacent rock and contact-stress neighboring corals. Allowing generous spacing around the colony and treating its footprint like a slow-spreading mat helps avoid problems in a mixed reef layout.
breedingNotes: In the wild, Briareum asbestinum reproduces both sexually — through an annual release of gametes broadcast into the water column — and vegetatively, with detached fragments colonizing nearby substrate. In aquaria, propagation is typically achieved by cutting a section of the encrusting base or a finger lobe and securing it to a frag plug (a small ceramic or plastic disc used to mount coral cuttings) with cyanoacrylate gel. Fragments commonly reattach and resume polyp extension within several days under good conditions.

primary.name: Reeflings — Corky Sea Fingers (Briareum asbestinum) Complete Care Guide
primary.notes: Used after the assigned LiveAquaria primary (https://www.liveaquaria.com/products/sea-stalk-briareum) returned HTTP 404. Source provides temperature 24–28 °C / 75–82 °F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, lighting 50–150 PAR, gentle-to-moderate flow, semi-aggressive reef compatibility (may overgrow neighbors), photosynthetic with zooxanthellae, moderate-to-easy care level, Caribbean origin, easy aquarium propagation.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Briareum asbestinum
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Briareidae, Scleralcyonacea, Octocorallia), Caribbean / Bahamas / Florida distribution, depth range 1–40 m, adult colony size ~30 cm typical with fingers reaching up to ~1 m in exceptional specimens, color range (purple/grey/tan/brown/greenish-brown), hairy polyp morphology, and dual sexual + vegetative reproduction. Confirms erect cylindrical unbranched finger growth form.

additional.name: Reeflex — Briareum asbestinum
additional.notes: Cross-checked colony size up to ~100 cm, depth 1–55 m, West Atlantic distribution (Caribbean, Florida, Belize, Panama, Gulf of Mexico), minimum tank ~22 gal (~100 L), care level easy, photosynthetic + planktivore diet.

additional.name: TankFacts — Corky Sea Finger
additional.notes: Cross-checked full water-chemistry targets: temperature 76–80 °F (24–27 °C), pH 7.8–8.4, SG 1.023–1.026, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, calcium 380–450 ppm, magnesium 1200–1400 ppm, nitrate 1–20 ppm, phosphate 0.01–0.1 ppm. Confirmed erect finger-cylinder morphology and purple-to-tan coloration with grass-like brown-green polyps.

additional.name: WoRMS — Briareum asbestinum (Pallas, 1766)
additional.notes: Taxonomic authority: family Briareidae, order Scleralcyonacea, class Octocorallia. Confirms Pallas 1766 as authority and lists numerous historical synonyms (Alcyonium asbestinum, Briareum gorgonoideum, Erythropodium polyanthes, etc.).
